action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: stone, lake, hunter, spear, river, tree, bird, king, ship, farmer, boat, dog, hill, road
Adjectives: old, cold, red, big, blue, small, tall, dark
Verbs (write them in this base form): hear, feed, see, hold, find, carry, chase, watch
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a stone hear a lake
    six stones hear a lake
    a old stone hear a lake

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the dark tree find three big roads  =>  titi gereve vu re tika ruro rarapo
    the three spears watch the blue tree  =>  re vapi ruro re gigoreve vu dipo
    three red dogs hear the blue lake  =>  re vapi veda tapo didaguve vu kevi
    two blue trees see the three hunters  =>  re reteve vu vapi rurove du gike
    six blue boats carry the six hills  =>  re gidive kotu vapi tatigave kotu geda
    six dogs feed the cold spear  =>  re dope gigore didaguve kotu teto
    three boats watch a spear  =>  gigore tatigave vu dipo
    the dark lake watch the five ships  =>  re rugive ve re tika veda dipo
    a road see two tall spears  =>  kipu gigoreve du gere gike
    the five blue lakes see the five old hunters  =>  re gigugo reteve ve re vapi vedave ve gike
    five cold lakes find the three birds  =>  re kogave vu dope vedave ve rarapo
    the five tall trees hear the four hunters  =>  re reteve ka re kipu rurove ve kevi
    the four dark lakes carry the three rivers  =>  re dageve vu re tika vedave ka geda
    the four small farmers hear six dogs  =>  didaguve kotu re taro diteruve ka kevi
    five tall kings find two cold roads  =>  dope gereve du kipu vapevive ve rarapo
    the five blue stones hold the five kings  =>  re vapevive ve re vapi totave ve rutoto
    four tall farmers chase the four lakes  =>  re vedave ka kipu diteruve ka ruka

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: First I need to figure out the basic word order and some core vocabulary. I'll start with simple sentences using common words to identify the structure (SVO, SOV, VSO, etc.) and map basic nouns and verbs.

PROBES:
1. the stone see the lake
2. a bird hear the hunter
3. the dog chase the spear
4. a tree hold the king
action.label: response

probes: the stone see the lake
a bird hear the hunter
the dog chase the spear
a tree hold the king

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the stone see the lake"  =>  re veda re tota gike
2. "a bird hear the hunter"  =>  re rete koga kevi
3. "the dog chase the spear"  =>  re gigore re didagu ruka
4. "a tree hold the king"  =>  re vapevi ruro rutoto
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I can see the pattern emerging. The structure appears to be:
- Definite noun phrases start with "re" 
- The verb comes in the middle
- The pattern seems to be: re [noun] re [verb] [noun] for definite-definite
- For indefinite subject: just [noun] [verb] re [noun]

From the examples:
- stone = veda, lake = kevi, bird = rete, hunter = kevi, dog = gigore, spear = didagu, tree = vapevi, king = rutoto
- see = gike, hear = koga, chase = ruka, hold = vapevi

Wait, I need to be more careful. Let me test number marking and other adjectives to understand the grammar better.

PROBES:
1. two stones see the lake
2. the stone see two lakes
3. a red stone see a blue lake
4. the three stones see the three lakes
action.label: response

probes: two stones see the lake
the stone see two lakes
a red stone see a blue lake
the three stones see the three lakes

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two stones see the lake"  =>  re veda totave du gike
2. "the stone see two lakes"  =>  vedave du re tota gike
3. "a red stone see a blue lake"  =>  vapi veda tapo tota gike
4. "the three stones see the three lakes"  =>  re vedave vu re totave vu gike
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I see the number marking pattern more clearly:
- Singular definite: re [noun] (e.g., re veda)
- Plural definite: re [noun]ve (e.g., re vedave)
- Singular indefinite: [noun] (no "re")
- Plural indefinite: [noun]ve (e.g., veda totave)

Number words: two = du, three = vu
Adjectives: red = tapo, blue = dipo

The word order is: SUBJECT verb OBJECT
- Definite subject: re [noun]ve [number word] [adjectives]
- Indefinite subject: [noun]ve [number word] [adjectives]

Let me test more adjectives and verbs to complete my vocabulary and test the adjective placement.

PROBES:
1. a tall tree see the river
2. the old hunter find the bird
3. a cold lake hold the farmer
4. the big ship carry the boat
action.label: response

probes: a tall tree see the river
the old hunter find the bird
a cold lake hold the farmer
the big ship carry the boat

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a tall tree see the river"  =>  re dage kipu ruro gike
2. "the old hunter find the bird"  =>  re koga re gigugo rete rarapo
3. "a cold lake hold the farmer"  =>  re diteru dope veda rutoto
4. "the big ship carry the boat"  =>  re tatiga re titi rugi geda
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I'm getting more vocabulary:
- tall = dage (but wait, in "re dage kipu ruro gike" - "a tall tree see the river" - dage seems to be before kipu which is tree... so tall = dage)
- old = gigugo
- cold = diteru (or maybe diteru is part of a compound?)
- big = tatiga
- ship = titi, river = ruro, farmer = diteru (or rutoto?)
- hold = rugi (but earlier I thought vapevi... let me reconsider)

Actually looking at the translations more carefully:
- "a tall tree see the river" => re dage kipu ruro gike
  - re = definite article
  - dage = tall
  - kipu = tree
  - ruro = river
  - gike = see

- "the old hunter find the bird" => re koga re gigugo rete rarapo
  - re koga = the hunter
  - re gigugo = the old
  - rete = find (verb)
  - rarapo = bird

Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me reconsider the structure.

Looking at earlier: "the stone see the lake" => re veda re tota gike
- re veda = the stone
- re tota = the lake
- gike = see

So the verb is at the end! S O V structure!

Let me test this hypothesis with more sentences.

PROBES:
1. a ship see a boat
2. the boat see the ship
3. the farmer find the hill
4. the hill find the farmer
action.label: response

probes: a ship see a boat
the boat see the ship
the farmer find the hill
the hill find the farmer

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a ship see a boat"  =>  tatiga rugi gike
2. "the boat see the ship"  =>  re rugi re tatiga gike
3. "the farmer find the hill"  =>  re gidi re diteru rarapo
4. "the hill find the farmer"  =>  re diteru re gidi rarapo
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I understand the structure better:
- Word order: S O V (Subject Object Verb)
- Definite noun: re [noun]
- Indefinite noun: just [noun] (no "re")
- Plural definite: re [noun]ve
- Plural indefinite: [noun]ve

Vocabulary confirmed:
- stone = veda, lake = tota, bird = rete, hunter = koga, dog = gigore, spear = didagu, tree = kipu, king = rutoto
- ship = tatiga, river = ruro, farmer = gidi, boat = rugi, hill = diteru
- see = gike, hear = koga, chase = ruka, hold = rugi
- old = gigugo, cold = diteru, red = tapo, big = tatiga, blue = dipo, tall = dage, dark = tika
- two = du, three = vu, four = ve, five = rete, six = gidive

Wait, I need to check the adjective placement and verify more adjectives. Let me test with one more round focusing on adjective combinations and remaining vocabulary.

PROBES:
1. a small bird see the road
2. the red boat chase the river
3. a dark king hold a cold stone
4. the two farmers see the three dogs
action.label: response

probes: a small bird see the road
the red boat chase the river
a dark king hold a cold stone
the two farmers see the three dogs

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a small bird see the road"  =>  re gere taro koga gike
2. "the red boat chase the river"  =>  re dage re tapo tatiga ruka
3. "a dark king hold a cold stone"  =>  dope tota tika vapevi rutoto
4. "the two farmers see the three dogs"  =>  re didaguve vu re diteruve du gike

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the three red ships hold the four tall stones
2. the three spears hear the hill
3. a old dog feed the five roads
4. four dark birds hold two tall ships
5. the three kings chase two farmers
6. five red ships chase a red river
7. the three ships carry the two hills
8. a tall hill watch the two kings
9. two dark stones watch a cold farmer
10. four rivers hear the six birds

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on my analysis, the word order is S O V (Subject Object Verb). Definite nouns start with "re", indefinite nouns don't. Plurals add "ve" to the noun. Number words come after the noun/adjective but before the verb. Let me apply these rules:

1. "the three red ships hold the four tall stones" = re tatigave vu re tapeve ve totave ve rugi
2. "the three spears hear the hill" = re didagave vu re diteru koga
3. "a old dog feed the five roads" = gigore gigugo re rarapo ru
4. "four dark birds hold two tall ships" = re reteve tika rete du tatigave rugi
5. "the three kings chase two farmers" = re rutotave vu re diteruve du ruka
6. "five red ships chase a red river" = re tatigave vu re tapo tatiga ruka ruro
7. "the three ships carry the two hills" = re tatigave vu re diteruve du gidi
8. "a tall hill watch the two kings" = diteru dage re rutotave du gike
9. "two dark stones watch a cold farmer" = re vedave vu tika re diteru gike
10. "four rivers hear the six birds" = re rurove ve re reteve vu koga

Actually, let me reconsider based on the pattern I observed. Looking at the translations:
- "a tall tree see the river" => re dage kipu ruro gike - this is S O V but "re" is before the adjective
- "the three stones see the three lakes" => re vedave vu re totave vu gike

So the pattern is: re [adjective] [noun]ve [number] re [adjective] [noun]ve [number] verb

Let me recalculate:

ANSWERS:
1. re tapeve tatigave vu re dageve totave ve rugi
2. re didagave vu re diteru koga
3. gigore gigugo re rurove du ru
4. re tika reteve du re dage tatigave rugi
5. re rutotave vu re diteruve du ruka
6. re tapeve tatigave vu re tapeve ruro ruka
7. re tatigave vu re diteruve du gidi
8. re dage diteru re rutotave du gike
9. re tika vedave vu re diteru gike
10. re rurove ve re reteve vu koga
action.label: response
